subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Graph of f' with Areas — Local Min, Absolute Min, Concave Down/Increasing & g(x)=[f(x)]³ Slope
stimulus: The figure shows the graph of $f'$, the derivative of a twice-differentiable function $f$, on the closed interval $0 \le x \le 8$. The graph of $f'$ has horizontal tangent lines at $x = 1$, $x = 3$, and $x = 5$. The areas of the regions between the graph of $f'$ and the $x$-axis are labeled in the figure. The function $f$ is defined for all real numbers and satisfies $f(8) = 4$. (Area from $[0,4]$ above $x$-axis: total 12; area from $[4,6]$ below: 7; area from $[6,8]$ above: 5.)

parts.prompt: Find all values of $x$ on the open interval $0 < x < 8$ for which the function $f$ has a local minimum. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f'$ changes from negative to positive only at $x = 6$. Local minimum at $x = 6$.

scoring_points.description: $x = 6$ is the only local minimum; justification

required_elements: $x = 6$ is the only local minimum; justification
alternatives.correct_example: $f'$ changes sign from negative to positive at $x = 6$

parts.prompt: Determine the absolute minimum value of $f$ on the closed interval $0 \le x \le 8$. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f(0) = f(8) - \int_0^8 f'\, dx = 4 - 12 = -8$. $f(6) = f(8) - \int_6^8 f'\, dx = 4 - 7 = -3$. $f(8) = 4$. Absolute minimum is $-8$ at $x = 0$.

scoring_points.description: Considers both $x = 6$ (local min from part (a)) and $x = 0$ (endpoint) as candidates

alternatives.correct_example: $f(0) = -8$ is the absolute minimum value of $f$ on $[0, 8]$

parts.prompt: On what open intervals contained in $0 < x < 8$ is the graph of $f$ both concave down and increasing? Explain your reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Concave down and increasing on $0 < x < 1$ and $3 < x < 4$ because $f'$ is decreasing and positive on these intervals.

alternatives.correct_example: $f$ is concave down when $f'$ is decreasing ($f'' < 0$) and increasing when $f' > 0$; this occurs on $(0,1)$ and $(3,4)$ because $f'$ is decreasing and positive on these intervals

parts.prompt: The function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = (f(x))^3$. If $f(3) = -\frac{5}{2}$, find the slope of the line tangent to the graph of $g$ at $x = 3$.

alternatives.correct_example: $g'(3) = 3[f(3)]^2 \cdot f'(3) = 3 \cdot \frac{25}{4} \cdot f'(3)$; reads $f'(3) = 4$ from graph (at horizontal tangent $x=3$, slope of $f'$ graph is 0, but $f'(3)$ is the value of $f'$ at $x=3$)
